data.title: Cabaletta Bio: Do You Buy With The Insiders?
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I've maintained a steady, cautious optimism for Cabaletta Bio (CABA) as they have tried to march an engineered cell therapy through the clinical trial pipeline in the field of autoimmune disease. In my last coverage back in October, I highlighted concerns I had about cash runway, but still came away with a positive impression based on the promise of then-upcoming data releases. Today, I want to digest what we've learned since then and make the case for continued optimism following the news that insiders have made [modest] share purchases.

Cabaletta Bio Pipeline Updates

Resecabtagene autoleucel (CABA-201)

I've covered what we've learned about CABA's CD19-directed CAR T-cell therapy rese-cel in past articles, with emphasis on what we learned in autoimmune disease coming out of the EULAR 2025 Congress. Please check that out if you want to learn more about past updates.

Rather, let's focus for now on the update that CABA was telegraphing for much of 2025: an update of findings from the RESET suite of trials. Particularly, at the ACR convergence in late October, a complete readout of RESET-Myositis was presented in an oral presentation, showcasing outcomes for 13 patients who received rese-cel for one of several eligible forms of myositis. No ICANS was observed, and some patients experienced cytokine release syndrome (4 of 13), all grade 1.

There were 4 patients with dermatomyositis or antisynthetase syndrome who met eligibility for the ongoing registrational cohort of RESET-Myositis. All of these patients had a moderate or major improvement in immunomodulatory-free total improvement score at week 16.

The now-ongoing registrational cohort of RESET-Myositis will enroll 17 patients with a primary endpoint of major or moderate improvement at week 16, in alignment with discussions with the FDA about the design of a trial that should be sufficient to pursue approval. CABA now guides that, if successful, this trial could serve as the basis for a submission to the FDA in 2027.

In a similar vein, CABA has announced that they've reached alignment with the FDA on the design of registrational cohorts for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and lupus nephritis (LN), and the company is planning two different cohorts enrolling 25 patients with each of these diseases, with further updates to come as 2026 progresses. In a preliminary report of the RESET-SLE trial, a group of 9 patients with SLE or LN had favorable rates of cytokine release syndrome (again, all grade 1) and only one instance of ICANS. 4 patients in each disease group had at least 3 months of follow-up, and 75% in each group achieved remission or renal response. All 9 patients who had received rese-cel were receiving no immunomodulators at the time of data cutoff.

Next, CABA is going to expand the RESET-SLE trial with a dose-expansion cohort that receives weight-based dosing of rese-cel without preconditioning using fludarabine and cyclophosphamide (ie, a regimen designed to deplete the body's immune cells to allow for the CAR T-cells to expand and reconstitute the immune system from scratch). Data from this dose expansion cohort are anticipated in 2026.

CABA Stock Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, CABA held $60.2 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $99.7 million in short-term investments. Operating expenses reached $46.6 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $44.9 million. In the first 9 months of 2025, CABA used $95.7 million in cash to fund operations, leaving a cash runway of approximately 5 quarters, consistent with management guidance that they could continue operations at these levels "into the second half of 2026."

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Nearing critical milestones in clinical development

Although their studies are modest in size, CABA has generated some rather exciting efficacy in lupus and myositis, as summarized above. And exploring the use of rese-cel without preconditioning potentially opens the door to treatment imposing fewer risks to patients. The fact that CABA has a legitimate shot at approval in 2027 is definitely something to get excited about, considering the number of companies in play with engineered cell therapies for autoimmune disease. In short, 2026 should be a very interesting year on the data front.

Risk - The cash situation is about to become dire without some kind of intervention

Unfortunately, although CABA's cash position appears fairly sturdy at first glance, their cash burn is rather high in relation. Within a quarter or two, I would expect the company to be looking to make as definitive a move on the fundraising front as possible. For a cell therapy company, I would expect this to come in the form of a public offering, and the amount they can raise without incurring massive dilution will depend on their ability to elevate the market's interest, which is no small feat in this day and age where cell therapy companies, by and large, have struggled to gain traction.

Bottom-Line Summary

An investment thesis for CABA has several big factors working against it. First, the cash on hand will likely not be sufficient to carry them to a potential commercial launch, not even in the smoothest, fastest timeline to approval. That creates an overhang for the market that expects a large dilutive equity raise at any moment.

Second, cell therapies for autoimmune disease remain decisively unproven. And it remains to be seen what the actual clinician and patient appetite is to incur the cost and potential risk of an autologous cell transplant for diseases outside the cancer space. Despite the market sizes for diseases like lupus and myositis measuring in the hundreds of millions to multiple billions of dollars, the relatively extreme approach of immune reset may put CAR T-cell therapy out of reach for all but the most severely affected patients. Time will tell whether CD19-directed cell therapy has a leg to stand on here, and then we'll need to see whether CABA can differentiate itself among many would-be contenders in the space.

It is also worth noting that the low average volume (average of 5800 shares daily according to Seeking Alpha) may make it difficult to enter and exit a position quickly, so I would definitely not be trading this one, despite the ability for CABA to move quickly.

Finally, on the topic of insider purchases, in general I don't personally subscribe to the notion that these mean much of anything, especially when the raw dollar amounts come to the tune of around $10,000 to $20,000 purchases. Many feel that these insider buys signal very high confidence in the data, or some kind of tell about yet-undisclosed positive data, but in my observations over the years, for common shareholders, following this money trail has not been consistently fruitful. A notable, high-profile example was the purchase of shares of Summit Therapeutics by CEO Bob Duggan, which was read by many as a signal of imminent breakout of the stock.

But that breakout was a blip, and I am not convinced that the insider purchases have had anything to do with Summit's share price, outside of a very specific time window that generated excitement.

All that said, I am going to continue to affirm a "Buy" sentiment for CABA, but not on the basis of insider purchases. Rather, I am excited about the prospects for their potentially pivotal studies that may read out in 2026. They're generating momentum, and at a market cap of under $300 million at the time of writing, if they can make a clear play for approval, then they have the potential for substantial gains as they stand poised to be one of a few early pioneers in autoimmune cell therapy. I think it's a must-watch stock, even if volatility means there is likely going to be a more favorable entry point in the near term.

data.title: T1 Energy: Ascent Of Solar Made In America Could Face Icarus Moment (Upgrade)
data.description:
T1 Energy's (TE) surge over the last year could be set to collide with more bearish expectations from the market on profitability. The pivot from a Norwegian battery manufacturer looking to tap the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act to an Austin, Texas-based manufacturer and retailer of photovoltaic ("PV") solar modules in the U.S. has catalyzed a remarkable share price rally that has placed the ticker on a hockey stick ascent into the sun. TE is up 317% over the last year, with returns from its 52-week low at an even more substantial 785%.

TE's pivot, hinged on its late 2024 acquisition of the U.S. solar manufacturing assets of Trina Solar, has paid off well. The firm generated fiscal 2025 third-quarter revenue of $210.5 million, capping a metamorphosis that is set to benefit from a push by the White House to onshore much of the U.S. energy supply chains.

Tariffs have aggressively targeted non-U.S.-made PV solar modules, and TE's push to establish a vertically integrated domestic solar-plus-storage supply chain forms a tailwind within the current zeitgeist. The company is pushing to ramp up production at G1_Dallas, its 5 GW solar module facility. However, the rally looks overheated, profitability is currently elusive, and TE is now swapping hands at a $2.2 billion market cap. This rally has pulled in short sellers, with around 8% of the company's float being held short. The short argument hinges on three pillars. The first is that TE's rally has placed the security's valuation beyond its fundamentals. TE remains unprofitable with a gross profit margin that stood at 10% for its third quarter, as the firm generated gross profit of $21.15 million. Further, net loss from continuing operations was $127.6 million, a loss of around $0.85 per share. The company also held total debt of around $500 million against a cash and restricted cash position of $79.54 million as of the end of the third quarter.

Production Capacity, Share Sales, And Risks

Bulls would be able to point to the long-term performance of First Solar (FSLR) as a reason to remain long TE. FSLR is the largest domestic PV solar module manufacturer, with a $26 billion market cap. While TE focuses on silicon-based PV solar modules and represents more than 50% of this market, the demand for these will underpin its capital investment plan as management pushes to ramp up production capacity with G2_Austin. This will be developed in two stages, the first of which is for 2.1 GW and will require a capital expenditure of $400 million to $425 million. This capital need forms the second pillar of the short case. TE will be heavily dependent on the market to plug its capital expenditure liquidity gap as it continues to rack up heavy net losses from ongoing operations. The company actually raised $122 million in gross proceeds post-period end in October. I last covered TE in 2024 before the PV solar module pivot.

TE has a $500 million mixed securities shelf and, in December, announced underwritten public offerings of $120 million in convertible senior notes due 2030 and $140 million in common stock. However, the market has been lenient with its reaction to these dilutive share offerings, with the initial selloff from the December announcement met with a new high in the weeks after. This will almost certainly mean positive cadence with new offerings as TE seeks to deleverage its balance sheet and build an equity buffer for G2_Austin. The stock initially dipped around 15.4% following the December offering and has since hit a new 52-week high.

TE will likely need to raise more cash, with management possibly set to follow up on the December share offering with a further raise in the first half of 2026. Hence, there will likely be more short-term downside for the commons on the back of what's fundamentally a prudent decision to diversify the capital stack away from just debt. This comes as the company's G1_Dallas facility achieved a new daily production record of 14.4 MW in October, essentially equating to 5.2 GW of annualized production.

Conclusion

The third pillar of the bears is more technical, hinged on the common shares being likely to see some more sustained profit taking after their immense rally. Bears have been wrong for most of the last year, and TE still faces a few positive catalysts as demand for energy continues to ramp up on the back of the AI-driven boom in data center construction. This boom, in aggregate with tariffs on non-U.S. solar, has created an environment where TE has flown towards the sun. I am upgrading the ticker to a "Hold."

data.title: SCHO: Doesn't Pay For Its Duration
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

Exposure to the interest rate curve is one way to manage an investment portfolio defensively. To do so in an indexed manner, it is worth looking at the Schwab Short-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (SCHO). As its name suggests, the ETF provides exposure to the short end of the U.S. Treasury bond curve, specifically to bonds with maturities between 1 and 3 years. Its main objective is to replicate, before fees and expenses, the performance of the Bloomberg US Treasury 1–3 Year Index. This indicates that the ETF is managed passively, as it replicates the aforementioned index using the representative sampling technique.

My first approach to the investment thesis is to look at the ETF's performance and how we can use it in an investment portfolio. As I mentioned earlier, since this is a fixed-income ETF that invests in bonds with short maturities, we position it as a complement to the fixed-income portion of the portfolio, in the lower-risk segment. This should not exceed 10% of the total portfolio. With regard to the ETF's performance, the latest SEC Yield data gives us a return of (3.53%).

If we look at the current yield on 12-month Treasury bills, we see that it has been 3.40%. The difference between the two is 0.13% in favor of the ETF yield. What we need to see is whether that excess annual return offsets the duration risk assumed in the case of the ETF. From my point of view, and for defensive purposes and to “park” liquidity, the spread we have calculated is too narrow today, as even a slight movement in short-term rates could easily eliminate the extra carry offered by SCHO. Therefore, I recommend selling the ETF and transferring that allocation to 9-12 month Treasury bills. The yield spread does not justify the extra duration risk assumed by the ETF, so we are selling.

ETF Overview

The two images above show the main aspects of the ETF we are analyzing. It has obtained a 30-day SEC yield of 3.53% and a distribution yield over the last 12 months of 4.06%. The latter reflects the annualized cash payment received by the investor. The average duration is two years and the effective duration is 1.9 years. This means that the fund is not very sensitive to interest rate movements because it has little duration risk, as it is exposed to the section of the curve to which it is exposed (-1.9% in price for a 100 bp movement in the short end of the curve).

This comment fits in well with the statement I made in the previous section, where I said that the ETF had to justify the extra return it provides against the duration risk assumed. Considering that the extra return was 0.13% and the extra duration assumed is one year, for me, this extra exposure to the curve does not make sense for the purpose described above, since a small movement in short-term interest rates can wipe out the carry spread mentioned.

The Portfolio

The ETF portfolio consists of Treasury bonds with maturities ranging from one to three years. In the Security Type section, we see that 99.77% of the fund's assets are invested in securities issued by the US Treasury Department, confirming its total exposure to government debt (96.96% exposure to bonds). The credit rating section refers to the credit rating of these bonds held by the ETF. As they are US Treasury bonds with the aforementioned maturities, they have an AA rating (99.77%). And in the maturity breakdown section, we see that the bulk of the portfolio (99.77%) is in the 1-3 year maturity range, which is what we mentioned in the introductory section, and with this data, we can confirm it.

Payment Profile

The ETF payment letter is presented as definitive proof confirming our thesis. Although it is a payment letter with a very positive profile (A), I still maintain that it is more interesting to seek exposure to 1-year Treasury bills, especially if we take into account that there will be no interest rate cuts at the next FOMC meeting on January 28. Otherwise, we see that it has been paying dividends for 15 consecutive years, without any consecutive years of dividend growth. The dividend growth ratio over the last 12 months has been negative (-4.15%). This data fits perfectly with the macroeconomic situation experienced in 2025, when the Fed began to cut interest rates and the ETF's carry adjusted downward accordingly.

The Peers

Finally, I present a comparative assessment of the fund under review and its peers. In terms of fees, we see that SCHO, VGSH, and SPTS all have the same expense ratio of 0.03% per annum, which is very low for an expense. In the total return charts, we have, on the one hand, the total return of all ETFs over the last year (image 2) and the total return over the last three years (image 3). Over the last year, the proposed alternative (OBIL) does not outperform the ETF analyzed (SCHO), but it does so when we look at the three-year history.

Although this has occurred because the OBIL ETF, having a shorter duration, is better able to capture moments of interest rate rises (over the last three years), this does not detract from the fact that it has done so with less risk. Therefore, until the macro environment favors a consistent appreciation of the 1–3-year segment (i.e., a sustained decline in yields in that segment), my recommendation is to sell SCHO and use 9–12-month bills as a defensive instrument. Although there are very similar ETFs (VGSH, SPTS, SHY), they all share the same structural trade-off of assuming more duration than a 12-month bill, which is why I prefer bills for this portfolio objective. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Phillips 66: Finally Positioned For A Refining Up-Cycle (With A Venezuela Kicker)
data.description:
For many years, Phillips 66's (PSX) Refining Segment has underperformed relative to peers Valero Energy (VLO) and Marathon Petroleum (MPC). That fact is abundantly clear from the total returns comparison of the three largest independent refiners in the United States (see graphic below). However, it would appear that PSX's management team's promise to improve its poor refining margins is finally coming to fruition. That, combined with what is likely to be PSX's increased access to significantly discounted Venezuelan heavy crude, may well result in a relatively strong Refining Segment up-cycle for the company this year. Today, I'll take a fresh look at a company that I have long maintained a HOLD rating on (since July of 2023) to see if PSX just might be ready for an upgrade.

As you can see from the chart above, over the past 5 years - and despite a very strong midstream business - Phillips 66 has not come remotely close to delivering the very strong total returns of leading refiners like VLO and MPC.

Investment Thesis

The graphic below was taken from a Phillips 66 November Update Presentation and is a summary of the corporation's operations:

It's a busy slide, but basically PSX operates four business segments: Refining, Midstream, Chemicals, and Marketing & Specialties.

The company has eight refineries in the U.S. and two in Europe. The strength of its Refining Segment is in the Central Corridor of the United States because it has advantaged access to discounted oil sands crude and a very large coking capacity (i.e. distillate yield). Indeed, PSX is the largest importer of Canadian oil sands-based crude.

PSX's Midstream Segment is an integrated "wellhead-to-market" organization which includes NGLs logistics (gathering and processing), long-haul NGL pipelines, and significant fractionation capacity on the Gulf Coast that serve the chemicals and global LPG export markets.

PSX operates chemical operations primarily through a 50/50 joint venture with Chevron (CVX) called CPChem. CPChem has 31 chemical plants around the world, with the majority of its capacity along the U.S. Gulf Coast, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, China, and Singapore.

In the Marketing & Specialties Segment (M&S), PSX in aggregate has close to 9,000 U.S. and international retail outlets with the highly recognizable Phillips 66, Conoco, 76, and JET brands.

For years now, the Midstream and M&S Segments have been the company's most steadily performing businesses, with the refinery business' performance being inconsistent (at best) and the Chemicals Segment being, well, a disaster in my opinion. I have written about how Exxon (XOM) and CPChem have been on a race to the bottom by wasting literally $10s of billions of shareholder capital on new and massive global petrochemical plants that significantly added capacity to an already well-supplied (or even over-supplied ...) global market. See "Phillips 66: Q2 Results Will Show, Once Again, Weak Chemicals Margins". As a result, and despite billions of investments to add additional chemicals capacity, PSX often posts Chemicals Segment earnings that are actually below the earnings of the segment prior to that new capacity coming online. That, combined with PSX's lagging refining operations, was the primary reason I went to a HOLD on the stock in July of 2023 and I've maintained that rating ever since.

However, and although there are still challenges in the Chemicals Segment going forward, there now appears to be light at the end of the tunnel with regard to the company's significant improvement initiatives to improve the Refining Segment. Let's take a look.

The Refining Segment

I've been a long-term shareholder in Phillips 66, having received my shares due to the original spin-off from ConocoPhillips (COP) back in 2012. After many years of Phillips 66 management's rhetoric about efforts to improve its significantly underperforming Refining Segment, I stopped listening to the talk and have been in a wait-and-see mode for the results to actually reflect the talk. And, after years of disappointment, positive and meaningful results are starting to emerge:

As you can see from the graphic above, last quarter (Q3FY25) PSX delivered the highest refinery crude utilization rate since 2018 (99%), a record YTD clean-product yield (up three percentage points from 2022), and has reduced controllable costs by $0.87/bbl. While at first glance $0.87/bbl may not sound like much, consider that PSX has ~2 million bpd of refining capacity, so that improvement adds up very quickly.

The result of PSX's Refining Segment improvements resulted in a Q3FY25 adjusted pre-tax profit of $430 million versus an adjusted pre-tax loss of $67 million in Q3FY24.

Phillips 66's "Central Corridor" refiners are well-named because they are central to the company's overall refining performance:

Phillips 66 has one of the highest distillate yields among its peer group. Indeed, as you can see from the upper-right-hand corner of this graphic, PSX has more coking capacity than any other refiner in the U.S. That being the case, PSX is the largest importer of discounted Canadian oil sands heavy crude. And, as reported earlier this month by CBC, following the upheaval in Venezuela:

A barrel of heavy oil from Western Canada is now selling at the widest discount compared to benchmark prices in North America since July 2024.

At the time of that article, Western Canada Select (WCS) traded at a $14.45/bbl discount to the value of West Texas Intermediate (WTI), the North American benchmark. That's a significant tailwind for PSX going forward.

In addition, last week it was reported on Seeking Alpha that PSX, along with Valero, purchased a cargo of Venezuelan heavy crude for its Gulf Coast refineries at a discount of between $8.50 and $9.50 a barrel to Brent crude. That certainly cuts down on the transportation costs of piping Canadian oil sands feedstock all the way from Canada to PSX's Gulf Coast refineries.

As that article pointed out, it doesn't take much additional waterborne heavy imports on the Gulf Coast to reflect up the supply chain to Canada. That being the case, it's not just PSX's Gulf Coast refineries that are going to benefit from increased Venezuelan imports; it's going to lower WCS feedstock costs for PSX's four mid-con refineries that are not on the Gulf Coast.

As I wrote in my recent Seeking Alpha article "The Big Picture: Thoughts On Oil And AI Cross-Pollination", I totally agree (for once) with what ExxonMobil CEO Darren Woods had to say about Venezuela during the infamous Friday meeting with President Trump's desire to rapidly expand oil production in the country:

We have had our assets seized there twice and so you can imagine to re-enter a third time would require some pretty significant changes from what we've historically seen and what is currently the state. Today it's uninvestable.

However, as I also said in that article, Chevron - the only U.S. major still operating in Venezuela - could see some short-term upside in its production due to the company already having boots on the ground and the country's economy almost entirely dependent on Chevron's success at producing oil for the global export market. Indeed, recent developments reported by Seeking Alpha indicate that Chevron is in talks with oil services companies SLB, Halliburton, and Baker Hughes. The initial focus is on repairing and/or replacing damaged or outdated equipment in order to revitalize older drilling operations and to quickly increase production. That would be a bullish development for Phillips 66 because the obvious destination for that increased production would be the Gulf Coast refineries that can best process it - and that also includes Valero and Chevron refineries as well.

Earnings

Phillips 66 is scheduled to report earnings on Feb. 3rd, before the market opens. The current consensus earnings estimate, courtesy of Yahoo.Finance is shown below:

As you can see from the graphic, the average analyst's earnings estimate for Q4 is currently $2.16/share, up $2.31 from a loss in the year-ago quarter. While full-year 2025 earnings are expected to come in only $0.19/share above FY24's result, note that the consensus earnings estimates for FY2026 are up significantly, to $11.16/share. If you scroll further down on the previously referenced Yahoo.Finance page, you will see that earnings estimates have been consistently rising over the past 90 days, and much of that is likely due to what I have discussed in this article: improved Refining Segment results and expectations for a Venezuela "kicker".

Valuation

PSX currently trades with a TTM P/E = 38.2x and a yield of 3.4%. Seeking Alpha is reporting a forward P/E = 22.3x. The forward P/E metric appears rational given the current state of play with PSX relative to the broader refining market.

One reason I have held onto PSX so long is the excellent dividend growth it has demonstrated. On that front, PSX's current yield is in the middle of its recent range - I say that because, in my opinion, you can disregard the pandemic dislocation.

Speaking of valuation, while I agree with Seeking Alpha's Factor Grade on Valuation (a C-), I don't understand the Seeking Alpha's Dividend Grade:

PSX's 15% CAGR, demonstrated over more than a decade, certainly deserves better than a "C+". Indeed, I'm sure there are a lot of investors who would enjoy a 15% CAGR in dividend increases - like Exxon, for example - that has been averaging around a 3% dividend growth rate for many years, yet Exxon is given a B+ for dividend growth?

Risks

PSX is a commodity-driven stock and is therefore exposed to the macro global economic environment and to the supply/demand fundamentals of the oil, gas, and NGLs markets. In particular, with PSX's decision to increase fractionation capacity, the company has become more reliant on LPG exports and increasing domestic demand.

Recently, natural gas prices have jumped, and natural gas is a significant feedstock for PSX's refineries and chemical operations. That being the case, a higher price for natural gas generally reduces profits in those segments.

From a balance sheet perspective, and due primarily to Phillips 66's recent EPIC NGL acquisition combined with taking over 100% of the Wood River and Borger refineries (i.e., buying out Cenovus's (CVE) interests), PSX's net-debt-to-capital ratio of 41% is very high in comparison to Valero's 11%. However, PSX has already taken action on this front with the JET disposition, which closed on December 1st and which PSX CFO Keven Mitchell discussed on the Q3 conference call:

In the fourth quarter of this year, we will have the proceeds from the JET disposition, but we also just funded the WRB acquisition, and those 2 kind of offset, but we'll have a sizable working capital benefit in the fourth quarter, somewhere in the order of $1.5 billion will come back to us, maybe slightly more.

This is something for shareholders to watch, although PSX does have a solid track-record of reducing debt following acquisitions.

PSX stock has already appreciated due, in part, to the ongoing developments in Venezuela and the general impression that PSX will be on the receiving end of greater volumes of heavy oil from the country (see chart at the end of the article). That is a risk because it may not work out as anticipated.

Upside risks include a continuation of a falling U.S. dollar, which generally pushes energy prices higher, as well as total ownership of the Wood River and Borger refineries, which increases PSX's share of profits from both just as the developments with respect to Venezuela have widened the discount of the WCS discount of both refineries feedstock.

Summary & Conclusions

PSX is certainly showing some improvement in its refining operations. However, the stock has already had a significant rally, and the valuation level and dividend yield do not present that much of a bargain, in my opinion. Also, call me gun-shy, but I am still in "wait-and-see" mode for PSX's Refining Segment to post consistently high returns relative to peers VLO and MPC. I also want to see PSX's debt level reduced, as it is a headwind to increased profitability and shareholder returns. Meantime, the Chemicals Segment is still underperforming, and that may continue for a year or two longer as the global market still appears to be overly supplied, pressuring chemicals margins.

Bottom line: I maintain my HOLD on PSX but deserve the right to upgrade the stock should the Q4 results demonstrate continued progress in the Refining Segment's operations and returns.

Second Opinions

However, I should report that just last week, Raymond James raised its price target on PSX to $175, and Goldman Sachs raised its price target to $164. The average of those two price targets is $169.50, which would equate to a 12-month gain of 20.5% based on the current trading price of $140.66. Add in the 3.4% dividend yield, and you're starting to close in on a 25% annual total return. If I thought $169.50 was a rational price target, I'd put a buy on the stock too. But, like I said, I am in "wait-and-see" mode.

I'll end with a 10-year total returns chart comparing PSX with refining peers VLO and MPC, as well as its JV partner Chevron and the broader energy sector as represented by the State Street Energy Sector SPDR ETF (XLE):

As you can see from the graphic, and despite PSX's excellent dividend growth since the spin-off in 2012 from ConocoPhillips, the stock is at the very bottom of the energy companies chosen for this comparison—even underperforming the XLE Energy ETF by 10%.

data.title: First Eagle Overseas Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

4th Quarter 2025

MSCI World Index

+3.12%

MSCI EAFE Index

+4.86%

S&P 500 Index

+2.66%

German DAX Index

+2.55%

French CAC 40 Index

+3.62%

Nikkei 225 Index

+12.18%

Brent Crude Oil

-9.21%

$60.85 a barrel

Gold

+11.93%

$4,319.37 an ounce

$4,319.37 an ounce

+6.13% vs. yen

+0.05% vs. euro

Source: Bloomberg, WM/Reuters.

Market Overview

Risk assets in general capped off a strong year with a solid fourth quarter. In contrast with recent trends, non-US equity markets led the way in 2025.

While the threats to market stability that have prevailed throughout the year were undiminished during the quarter, investors continued to embrace risk. The S&P 500 Index gained 2.7% for the fourth quarter and 17.9% for the year, while the MSCI EAFE Index returned a respective 4.9% and 31.2%, Notably, 2025 was only the third year in the past 10 that the MSCI EAFE has outperformed the S&P 500. Gold, meanwhile, surged 65% during the year, its largest annual return since 1979.1

The US Double Bind Gets Tighter

Despite ample motivation for conservatism in an environment of pronounced macro, financial, geopolitical and structural concerns, risk perception in US markets remains low pretty much wherever you look. Equity market valuation multiples are rich, high yield spreads are tight and implied volatility is low.2

While the post-pandemic normalization of certain macro factors has been encouraging, the country’s fiscal settings remain far off-kilter. The US federal deficit remains historically outsized relative to the unemployment rate—as it has since the outbreak of Covid-19.3 Normally, high unemployment rates and recession beget large fiscal deficits, as lower tax revenues combine with increased government spending. Conversely, low unemployment rates and robust economic growth typically support higher tax revenues and tighter fiscal policy, causing deficits to contract or even turn into surpluses. If the economy were in balance, we’d expect budget deficits of around 2% of GDP—not the 5.8% at the end of fiscal year 2025.4

We believe this persistent deficit spending helps explain the decoupling of gold and Treasuries seen in recent years. The price of gold surged 65% during 2025—its largest annual gain since 1979—and has more than doubled over the past two years in an apparent acknowledgement of the double-bind facing US policymakers: Doing nothing to address the deficit could stoke renewed inflationary pressures, while taking action to curb it would likely increase the possibility of recession. More recent rallies in the prices of other precious metals like silver and platinum appear to reflect the same policy conundrum.5

The US is not alone in this regard, of course, as fiscal deterioration has been widespread across both advanced and emerging economies.

However, the US is among only a few key economies—alongside the UK and Brazil—facing twin deficits, and this combination of negative fiscal and current account balances represents an incremental risk that most others do not face.6 The US current account deficit reflects an imbalance between savings and investment in the economy, which, by formula, must be offset by inflows of foreign capital into the US. The current account deficit is not necessarily a bad thing; the US has long been a popular destination for foreign investment, bolstered by the dollar’s status as the global reserve currency. However, it does complicate efforts to consolidate fiscal policy.

Twin deficits are nothing new for the US, which has run them consistently since the early 1980s with only a few exceptions, the most recent being 2001.7 More often than not, however, the fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP has exceeded the current account deficit; efforts to bring the fiscal deficit to levels less than that of the current account deficit have the potential to bleed into the private sector, impacting free cash flow and causing corporate credit issues.8

Beyond this fiscal reckoning is a question of how long the tailwinds that have supported both economic and equity market growth in recent years can persist. Chief among these has been the massive spending on the buildout of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Spending on semiconductor fabrication and data centers on average have accounted for 0.4% of GDP growth annually since 2022, and the growth of technology investment overall has contributed nearly half of GDP growth in recent quarters.9

Capital expenditures by hyperscalers—companies like Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) that operate massive data centers supporting cloud computing—have grown rapidly over the past decade or so, at a pace far exceeding that of cash flow from operations.10 As a result, capex as a percentage of cash flow from operations for these companies has grown from about 20% in 2015 to 70% today, and what had been very free-cash-flow generative businesses are now decidedly less so.11 Spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure by hyperscalers is forecast to continue, but its current rate of growth is unsustainable absent some other sources of financing. To us, it seems likely to decelerate toward the growth rate of operating cash flow for these companies, which may represent an unwelcome plot twist in the market’s AI narrative.

Finding Ballast Across Assets

Already-high geopolitical tensions ratcheted up a notch in early 2026, as the US took military action on Venezuelan soil to remove President Nicolas Maduro. The Trump administration has publicly offered a range of justifications for forcing leadership change in Venezuela— including the illegitimacy of the elections that brought Maduro to power, the country’s role in the international drug trade and the seizure of US oil interests, among others. To us, one clear, if unspoken, goal of the operation was to check the influence of China and Russia on Venezuela and Latin America in general. Both nations have close diplomatic and economic ties in the region and staunchly oppose US dominance.

While escalations such as we have seen in Latin America in recent weeks are largely unpredictable, they are not surprising amid a geopolitical order in flux. We’ve spoken in recent years about the emergence of the Eurasian heartland, with authoritarian powers concentrated in eastern Europe and Asia—China, Russia, Iran and North Korea— growing increasingly aligned. More recently, the behavior of the US, long seen as reliable partner to like-minded countries worldwide, has led many to question the durability of its traditional alliances.

Geopolitics is a complex system, and the current disequilibrium has increased the possibility of destabilizing left-tail events—be they in the Americas, Europe or Asia.

Perhaps unsurprisingly in this uncertain environment, the monetary value of gold has been reasserting itself. Earlier, we noted the significant increase in the gold price over the past two years, but this rally has merely aligned gold with its 50-year geometric average relative to the stock of US public debt while bringing it closer to its geometric average versus the S&P 500.12 And though we’re attuned to the risk inherent in such a sharp price move, we continue to highly value its strategic hedge potential given the fiscal and geopolitical dynamics currently in place.

Gold, however, is not the only source of portfolio ballast. Nor is cash. In fact, one of our focuses in recent years has been on building portfolio resilience through equities we believe offer ballast though their lower risk character. This is not achieved simply through higher allocations to traditionally defensive segments of the market like health care and consumer staples, though we do have meaningful exposure to these sectors. Rather, we evaluate stocks across industries from the bottom up in search of attributes we believe contribute to low correlations to the broader market, including strong balance sheets, high margins, diverse product lineups, long-lived assets and contractually obligated revenues.

The resulting low-beta profile of our portfolios demonstrated that performance in the strong markets of 2025 does not require high correlation with benchmarks, and we believe it will serve clients well in markets that may be less accommodating going forward.

Portfolio Review

Overseas Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 5.78% in fourth quarter 2025. All regions contributed to performance; emerging markets and developed Europe were the leading contributors while developed Asia excluding Japan and Japan lagged. Materials, consumer staples and financials were the largest contributors by equity sector while communication services and energy detracted and consumer discretionary was flat. The Overseas Fund outperformed the MSCI EAFE Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Overseas Fund this quarter included gold bullion, FANUC Corporation (FANUY), Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF), LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton SE (LVMHF) and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. Pfd Non-Voting.

As noted in the Market Commentary, gold bullion continued to rally in the quarter on the back of such drivers as geopolitical uncertainty, strong demand from central banks and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and a dovish Federal Reserve.

Based in Japan, FANUC is a global leader in computerized numerical control devices and robots. Strong order growth in the quarter, particularly in the robot division, was supported by positive capex trends in the US and China. The market enthusiastically received FANUC’s expanded collaboration with Nvidia to bring “physical AI” (i.e., leveraging generative AI to convert language into robotic actions) into mainstream manufacturing. Finally, SoftBank’s accepted offer to purchase the robotics division of Swiss engineering firm ABB highlighted the value of factory automation assets.

Samsung Electronics is a global technology company and major manufacturer of diverse electronic components with a dominant presence in memory semiconductors. Results during the quarter reflect continued strong demand and shortage-induced pricing strength for DRAM chips and persistent demand from hyperscalers driven by AI infrastructure buildouts amid tight supply.

Headquartered in Paris, LVMH is the largest luxury goods business in the world. It produces and sells wine and cognac, perfumes and cosmetics, fashion and leather goods, and watches and jewelry, while also operating some retail outlets. Its portfolio of brands includes Louis Vuitton, Tiffany, Bulgari, Christian Dior, Sephora, Moët & Chandon and Hennessy. Following a period of relatively weak demand following the February announcement of tariffs by the US, the company reported strong sales during the most recent quarter driven by improved demand from China. LVMH is committed to price discipline on existing products while innovating new products.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Prosus N.V. Class N (PROSY), Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA), BAE Systems plc (BAESY), Imperial Oil Limited (IMO) and NAVER Corp. (NHNCF).

Prosus is a global technology company domiciled in Holland with a portfolio of private equity investments and an approximate 25% ownership stake in China’s publicly traded technology company Tencent, which is Prosus’s largest holding. Tencent shares traded down following strong performance for several quarters. However, it has been executing well and reported strong revenue and income growth for its most recent quarter. We also like Prosus’s ongoing shift away from early-stage venture investing toward more established, cash-generative business at reasonable multiples.

Shares of tech giant Alibaba traded down following several quarters of strong performance. The company continued to capitalize on the AI boom in China and reported accelerating sales growth in its cloud business for its most recent quarter. With large AI infrastructure and data centers and leading open-source and frontier large language models, the company has an attractive position in China’s AI ecosystem. Alibaba’s core e-commerce business continues to grow, but higher costs associated with large investments in quick commerce categories like food delivery weighed on the stock. We like Alibaba’s dominant market position, its strong execution capabilities and focus on returning cash to shareholders through dividends and stock repurchases.

BAE Systems, the largest defense contractor in the UK, traded lower following several quarters of strong performance. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively higher defense spending throughout Europe in the face of uncertain military support from the US, a commitment to which was underscored by the recent agreement among NATO members to increase their defense spending. BAE’s long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues that underpin strong backlogs and cyclical resilience.

Imperial Oil is a Canadian integrated oil company that is 70% owned by Exxon Mobil. Shares of Imperial Oil traded down alongside the decline in Brent oil prices and western Canadian oil prices. The company has been executing well and reported record crude production and strong refinery utilization for its most recent quarter. We continue to like Imperial Oil’s business quality, management’s record of prudent stewardship of capital and the company’s commitment to returning cash to shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Korean technology giant NAVER provides web portal services like search engine, online games, content development and online marketing services. The stock performed well in 2025 but gave back some gains in the fourth quarter. NAVER reported strong revenue growth for its most recent quarter but experienced some cost pressures from AI infrastructure investments. The company’s payment business also entered into an agreement to acquire Dunamu, Korea’s largest cryptocurrency exchange. We believe this transaction may potentially strengthen NAVER’s entrenchment in the Korean consumer ecosystem.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments
